items.question: Unit test vs integration test vs E2E test?
items.answer: **Unit test:** one function/class in isolation, ms, all collaborators mocked. **Integration test:** two or more real components together (e.g. parser + DB), 100s of ms, real I/O against a controlled environment. **E2E test:** the whole system end-to-end (UI → API → DB → external services), seconds-to-minutes, used sparingly. The test pyramid says: many at the bottom (unit), few at the top (E2E).

items.question: AAA pattern vs Given-When-Then?
items.answer: **AAA (Arrange / Act / Assert)** is the engineering vocabulary. **Given-When-Then** is the BDD vocabulary — same three phases under different names. Both surface the same intent: set up, do the thing, check the outcome. Use whichever your team prefers; consistency matters more than the choice.

items.question: Why do shared, mutable test fixtures cause flakiness?
items.answer: **Cause:** tests run in some order; if test_A mutates a shared object and test_B assumes its initial state, test_B's outcome depends on whether test_A ran first. **Effect:** the suite passes locally (where the order is one way) and fails in CI (where pytest may shuffle order or run in parallel). Symptom: 'works on my machine.' Fix: per-test fixtures with explicit scope, no module-level mutable state.

items.question: Why do unit tests catch fewer integration bugs than people expect?
items.answer: **Cause:** unit tests mock collaborators to keep the unit isolated. **Effect:** the mock can drift from reality (the real DB returns different errors than the mock; the real API has new fields). The unit looks correct against the mock and broken against the real thing. That's why you also need integration tests against the real contract.

items.question: What breaks if your unit tests sleep for 60 seconds to test a 1-minute backoff?
items.answer: **Perturbation:** real `time.sleep(60)` in the test. **Consequence:** suite runtime balloons; engineers stop running it locally; CI slows; soon nobody trusts or runs the test. Fix: inject the clock or use `freezegun` so the test 'sleeps' instantly. Real time has no business in unit tests.

items.question: What breaks if you assert on private implementation details (`_buffer`, `_cache`)?
items.answer: **Perturbation:** test asserts on `chunker._buffer == [...]`. **Consequence:** a harmless refactor (renaming `_buffer` to `_queue`, switching to a generator) breaks the test even though the behaviour is unchanged. Engineers either avoid refactoring (technical debt grows) or update the test (work that adds no value). Fix: assert only on what the caller observes via the public API.
